0.html:
The claude-second-brain CLI creates and manages brains registered in ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml. Run it via npx claude-second-brain, pnpm dlx claude-second-brain, or — if installed globally with npm i -g claude-second-brain — the shorter csb alias.

All examples below use claude-second-brain; substitute csb if you've installed it globally.
0.text:
The claude-second-brain CLI creates and manages brains registered in ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml. Run it via npx claude-second-brain, pnpm dlx claude-second-brain, or — if installed globally with npm i -g claude-second-brain — the shorter csb alias.
All examples below use claude-second-brain; substitute csb if you've installed it globally.
0.title: CLI Reference

1.html:
CommandDescription
claude-second-brainCreate a new brain (interactive).
claude-second-brain <name>Create a new brain named <name>, skipping the name prompt.
claude-second-brain lsList all registered brains. The default brain is marked with *.
claude-second-brain rm [<name>…]Delete one or more brains (directories + config.toml entries). Interactive multi-select if no name is given.
claude-second-brain path [flags]Print the path of the default (or named) brain.
claude-second-brain qmd [flags] -- …Run qmd against the default (or named) brain.
claude-second-brain helpShow usage.

ls is also available as list, and rm as remove.
1.text:
CommandDescriptionclaude-second-brainCreate a new brain (interactive).claude-second-brain <name>Create a new brain named <name>, skipping the name prompt.claude-second-brain lsList all registered brains. The default brain is marked with *.claude-second-brain rm [<name>…]Delete one or more brains (directories + config.toml entries). Interactive multi-select if no name is given.claude-second-brain path [flags]Print the path of the default (or named) brain.claude-second-brain qmd [flags] -- …Run qmd against the default (or named) brain.claude-second-brain helpShow usage.
ls is also available as list, and rm as remove.
1.title: Commands

2.html:
npx claude-second-brain                 # interactive — prompts for name + remote
npx claude-second-brain my-brain        # skip the name prompt

Creates the brain at ~/.claude-second-brain/<name>/, runs mise install, pnpm install, pnpm qmd:setup, git init, and registers it in ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml. The first brain created is set as the default.

During the interactive flow the CLI asks:

  • Brain name — defaults to my-brain.
  • Git remote — GitHub, Cloudflare Artifacts, or Skip. See GitHub and Cloudflare Artifacts.
  • Repo name — only shown when a remote is selected; defaults to the brain name.
  • Artifacts namespace — only shown for Cloudflare; defaults to default.
  • Register qmd collections now? — confirms running pnpm qmd:setup.
2.text:
npx claude-second-brain                 # interactive — prompts for name + remote
npx claude-second-brain my-brain        # skip the name prompt
Creates the brain at ~/.claude-second-brain/<name>/, runs mise install, pnpm install, pnpm qmd:setup, git init, and registers it in ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml. The first brain created is set as the default.
During the interactive flow the CLI asks:

Brain name — defaults to my-brain.
Git remote — GitHub, Cloudflare Artifacts, or Skip. See GitHub and Cloudflare Artifacts.
Repo name — only shown when a remote is selected; defaults to the brain name.
Artifacts namespace — only shown for Cloudflare; defaults to default.
Register qmd collections now? — confirms running pnpm qmd:setup.
2.title: Create a brain

3.html:
npx claude-second-brain ls

Prints each registered brain with its path, creation date, and git remote. The default brain is shown in bold with a * marker.
3.text:
npx claude-second-brain ls
Prints each registered brain with its path, creation date, and git remote. The default brain is shown in bold with a * marker.
3.title: List brains

4.html:
npx claude-second-brain rm my-brain             # single brain, confirmation prompt
npx claude-second-brain rm a b c                # remove multiple by name
npx claude-second-brain rm my-brain --yes       # skip confirmation + remote prompts
npx claude-second-brain rm                      # interactive multi-select picker

Deletes each brain's directory under ~/.claude-second-brain/ and removes its entry from config.toml. If you remove the default brain and others remain, the first remaining brain becomes the new default.

Interactive picker. When no name is passed, the CLI shows a multi-select list — press space to toggle a brain, a to toggle all, enter to confirm.

Remote deletion prompt. After the bulk confirmation, for each selected brain with a registered git_remote, the CLI asks whether to also delete the remote repo:

  • GitHub — runs gh repo delete <owner>/<repo> --yes (requires gh CLI with delete_repo scope).
  • Cloudflare Artifacts — calls DELETE /v1/api/namespaces/<ns>/repos/<repo> using CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN or a token fetched from wrangler auth token.

If a remote deletion fails (missing CLI, revoked token, etc.), the CLI prints the exact manual command and continues — the local directory and config entry are still removed.

Flags:

  • -y, --yes — skip all confirmation prompts, including the remote-deletion prompts. In -y mode remotes are left intact; delete them manually if needed.
4.text:
npx claude-second-brain rm my-brain             # single brain, confirmation prompt
npx claude-second-brain rm a b c                # remove multiple by name
npx claude-second-brain rm my-brain --yes       # skip confirmation + remote prompts
npx claude-second-brain rm                      # interactive multi-select picker
Deletes each brain's directory under ~/.claude-second-brain/ and removes its entry from config.toml. If you remove the default brain and others remain, the first remaining brain becomes the new default.
Interactive picker. When no name is passed, the CLI shows a multi-select list — press space to toggle a brain, a to toggle all, enter to confirm.
Remote deletion prompt. After the bulk confirmation, for each selected brain with a registered git_remote, the CLI asks whether to also delete the remote repo:

GitHub — runs gh repo delete <owner>/<repo> --yes (requires gh CLI with delete_repo scope).
Cloudflare Artifacts — calls DELETE /v1/api/namespaces/<ns>/repos/<repo> using CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN or a token fetched from wrangler auth token.

If a remote deletion fails (missing CLI, revoked token, etc.), the CLI prints the exact manual command and continues — the local directory and config entry are still removed.
Flags:

-y, --yes — skip all confirmation prompts, including the remote-deletion prompts. In -y mode remotes are left intact; delete them manually if needed.
4.title: Remove a brain

5.html:
npx claude-second-brain path                        # default brain root
npx claude-second-brain path --qmd                  # default brain's qmd index
npx claude-second-brain path --config               # ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml
npx claude-second-brain path --brain work --qmd     # a specific brain's qmd index

Flags:

  • --root (default) — the brain's directory (e.g. ~/.claude-second-brain/my-brain).
  • --qmd — the brain's qmd SQLite index (e.g. ~/.claude-second-brain/my-brain/.qmd/index.sqlite).
  • --config — the central config path. Ignores --brain.
  • --brain <name> — target a specific brain instead of the default.

Used by the global skills (/brain-ingest, /brain-search, /brain-refresh) to resolve paths at call time so they work from any working directory.
5.text:
npx claude-second-brain path                        # default brain root
npx claude-second-brain path --qmd                  # default brain's qmd index
npx claude-second-brain path --config               # ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml
npx claude-second-brain path --brain work --qmd     # a specific brain's qmd index
Flags:

--root (default) — the brain's directory (e.g. ~/.claude-second-brain/my-brain).
--qmd — the brain's qmd SQLite index (e.g. ~/.claude-second-brain/my-brain/.qmd/index.sqlite).
--config — the central config path. Ignores --brain.
--brain <name> — target a specific brain instead of the default.

Used by the global skills (/brain-ingest, /brain-search, /brain-refresh) to resolve paths at call time so they work from any working directory.
5.title: Print a path

6.html:
npx claude-second-brain qmd -- query -c wiki "distributed systems"
npx claude-second-brain qmd --brain work -- search -c wiki "kafka"

Forwards all arguments after -- to npx @tobilu/qmd, with INDEX_PATH set to the resolved brain's qmd index. Flags:

  • --brain <name> — target a specific brain instead of the default.
  • -- — end of CLI flags; everything after is passed to qmd verbatim.
6.title: Run qmd against a brain

7.html: Every command exits non-zero on failure (invalid brain name, missing config, etc.) and writes the error to stderr.
7.text: Every command exits non-zero on failure (invalid brain name, missing config, etc.) and writes the error to stderr.
7.title: Exit status

9.html:
• mise — auto-installed by the CLI if missing
  • Claude Code — the CLI that runs the wiki
  • Obsidian — optional but recommended

mise — auto-installed by the CLI if missing
Claude Code — the CLI that runs the wiki
Obsidian — optional but recommended
9.title: Requirements

10.html:
npx claude-second-brain

Or install globally for a shorter command (csb is an alias):

npm i -g claude-second-brain
csb                    # same as: claude-second-brain

The CLI will ask:

  • Brain name — what to call this brain (default: my-brain)
  • Git remote — optionally host your vault on GitHub (requires gh CLI) or Cloudflare Artifacts (requires a Cloudflare account + wrangler)

Creates the brain at ~/.claude-second-brain/my-brain/, registers it in ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml, installs mise + node + pnpm, runs pnpm install, and git init.

10.title: Step 1 — Scaffold

11.html:
cd ~/.claude-second-brain/my-brain && pnpm qmd:reindex

Generates local vector embeddings. First run downloads ~2GB of GGUF models — once.
11.text:
cd ~/.claude-second-brain/my-brain && pnpm qmd:reindex
Generates local vector embeddings. First run downloads ~2GB of GGUF models — once.
11.title: Step 2 — Generate vector embeddings

12.html:
If you created a GitHub repo during setup, it's already pushed — skip straight to opening in Obsidian.

If you skipped GitHub during setup, connect it now:

git remote add origin https://github.com/you/my-brain.git
git push -u origin main

Open ~/.claude-second-brain/my-brain/ as a vault in Obsidian — the folder is already a valid Obsidian vault. The Git plugin is pre-configured — enable it and sync is automatic.
12.text:
If you created a GitHub repo during setup, it's already pushed — skip straight to opening in Obsidian.
If you skipped GitHub during setup, connect it now:
git remote add origin https://github.com/you/my-brain.git
git push -u origin main
Open ~/.claude-second-brain/my-brain/ as a vault in Obsidian — the folder is already a valid Obsidian vault. The Git plugin is pre-configured — enable it and sync is automatic.
12.title: Step 3 — Open in Obsidian (and push to GitHub if not done)

13.html:
Run npx claude-second-brain (or csb if installed globally) again to create additional brains. Each gets its own folder under ~/.claude-second-brain/ and its own isolated qmd index. The global skills (/brain-ingest, /brain-search, /brain-refresh) always operate on the default brain in ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml. The first brain created is set as the default automatically.

See the CLI Reference for ls, rm, path, and qmd subcommands that manage and target specific brains.

13.title: Multiple brains

14.html:
Edit anywhere — Claude Code on desktop or mobile. Claude Code's GitHub integration lets you open the repo and work from anywhere — ingest a source, run a query, or update a page from your phone.

Read anywhere — Obsidian desktop and mobile. Open the repo as an Obsidian vault. The bundled .obsidian folder pre-configures obsidian-git — no setup required. Your wiki syncs automatically on every commit. For iOS: put the repo folder inside iCloud Drive — Obsidian Mobile picks it up natively.

Browse anywhere — GitHub. It's a plain GitHub repo. View and edit files directly in the browser at any time.
14.text:

Read anywhere — Obsidian desktop and mobile. Open the repo as an Obsidian vault. The bundled .obsidian folder pre-configures obsidian-git — no setup required. Your wiki syncs automatically on every commit. For iOS: put the repo folder inside iCloud Drive — Obsidian Mobile picks it up natively.
Browse anywhere — GitHub. It's a plain GitHub repo. View and edit files directly in the browser at any time.
14.title: Access from anywhere

15.html:
Your notes don't compound. This wiki does.

The fastest way to start your personal knowledge base powered by Obsidian, Claude Code, qmd, and GitHub.

npx claude-second-brain

One command gives you a fully wired knowledge system:

  • Claude ingests your sources and maintains a cross-linked wiki
  • qmd powers local semantic search (vector + BM25)
  • The scaffolded folder is your Obsidian vault — open it directly, with obsidian-git pre-configured for seamless sync
  • GitHub is the source of truth — version history, anywhere access, and a backup you control

You've been reading papers, articles, and books for years. Drop a source in, run /brain-ingest, and Claude reads it — extracts what matters, cross-links it to everything you already know, and files it. Ask a question six months later and get cited answers, not a list of files to re-read.

Inspired by Andrej Karpathy's approach to LLM-powered knowledge management — share an "idea file" with an LLM agent and let it build and maintain your knowledge base.
15.text:
Your notes don't compound. This wiki does.
The fastest way to start your personal knowledge base powered by Obsidian, Claude Code, qmd, and GitHub.
npx claude-second-brain
One command gives you a fully wired knowledge system:

Claude ingests your sources and maintains a cross-linked wiki
qmd powers local semantic search (vector + BM25)
The scaffolded folder is your Obsidian vault — open it directly, with obsidian-git pre-configured for seamless sync
GitHub is the source of truth — version history, anywhere access, and a backup you control

You've been reading papers, articles, and books for years. Drop a source in, run /brain-ingest, and Claude reads it — extracts what matters, cross-links it to everything you already know, and files it. Ask a question six months later and get cited answers, not a list of files to re-read.

Inspired by Andrej Karpathy's approach to LLM-powered knowledge management — share an "idea file" with an LLM agent and let it build and maintain your knowledge base.
15.title: claude-second-brain

16.html:
This is not a RAG system. It's not a chatbot over your notes. It's an actively maintained, cross-linked wiki — five structured page types, YAML frontmatter, and a set of Claude Code skills that run the whole thing.

The schema (CLAUDE.md) is that idea file. Claude reads it every session.
16.text:
This is not a RAG system. It's not a chatbot over your notes. It's an actively maintained, cross-linked wiki — five structured page types, YAML frontmatter, and a set of Claude Code skills that run the whole thing.
The schema (CLAUDE.md) is that idea file. Claude reads it every session.
16.title: How this is different

17.html:
ToolRole
Claude CodeReads sources, writes wiki pages, cross-links, flags contradictions
qmdLocal hybrid search (vector + BM25) across your entire wiki
ObsidianGraph view, backlinks, mobile reading — offline, no extra sync
GitHubSource of truth — version history, Claude Code anywhere, Obsidian sync

Everything is pre-configured. You bring the sources.

Next: Get started in 3 steps →

17.title: The stack

18.html: A running record of every released version of claude-second-brain on npm. Newest releases first. For auto-generated notes and tags, see the GitHub releases page.
18.text: A running record of every released version of claude-second-brain on npm. Newest releases first. For auto-generated notes and tags, see the GitHub releases page.
18.title: Ship log

19.html:
CLI:
  • Added claude-second-brain path [--brain N] [--root|--qmd|--config] and claude-second-brain qmd [--brain N] -- … subcommands. The global skills now resolve paths at call time via these commands instead of having absolute paths baked in at scaffold time.
  • config.toml's active field renamed to default. Existing configs with active = … are still read transparently and rewritten to default = … on the next write.
  • Global skills (brain-ingest, brain-search, brain-refresh) now ship with a .csb-version sidecar file. Re-running npx claude-second-brain <name> detects version mismatches and prompts before overwriting; CSB_SKIP_SKILL_UPDATES=1 disables the prompt.
  • Added csb as a bin alias. After npm i -g claude-second-brain, both claude-second-brain and csb are available globally.
Refactors:
  • Removed the __QMD_PATH__ and __CSB_CONFIG__ placeholder substitutions. scripts/qmd/{setup,reindex}.ts now compute the index path from import.meta.url; CLAUDE.md and vault-local skills use a relative .qmd/index.sqlite path.
19.text:
CLI:

Added claude-second-brain path [--brain N] [--root|--qmd|--config] and claude-second-brain qmd [--brain N] -- … subcommands. The global skills now resolve paths at call time via these commands instead of having absolute paths baked in at scaffold time.
config.toml's active field renamed to default. Existing configs with active = … are still read transparently and rewritten to default = … on the next write.
Global skills (brain-ingest, brain-search, brain-refresh) now ship with a .csb-version sidecar file. Re-running npx claude-second-brain <name> detects version mismatches and prompts before overwriting; CSB_SKIP_SKILL_UPDATES=1 disables the prompt.
Added csb as a bin alias. After npm i -g claude-second-brain, both claude-second-brain and csb are available globally.

Refactors:

Removed the __QMD_PATH__ and __CSB_CONFIG__ placeholder substitutions. scripts/qmd/{setup,reindex}.ts now compute the index path from import.meta.url; CLAUDE.md and vault-local skills use a relative .qmd/index.sqlite path.
19.title: v0.7.0 — 2026-04-17

20.html:
Features:
  • Added Cloudflare Artifacts as an alternate Git remote option during npx claude-second-brain setup. The CLI authenticates via wrangler, creates a repo via the Artifacts API, and pushes the initial commit automatically.
CLI UX:
  • qmd index path prompt now appears before the Git remote selection.
  • Global Claude skills are installed right after pnpm install, before git init. Install path is shown with ~/... notation.
  • qmd index prompt placeholder and default value display ~/... instead of the full home directory path.
Docs:
  • Added a Git backends section to the docs site covering GitHub and Cloudflare Artifacts setup.
  • Added llms.txt for LLM/AI crawler discoverability.
  • Docs site now generates .md files alongside HTML pages.
20.text:
Features:

Added Cloudflare Artifacts as an alternate Git remote option during npx claude-second-brain setup. The CLI authenticates via wrangler, creates a repo via the Artifacts API, and pushes the initial commit automatically.

Global Claude skills are installed right after pnpm install, before git init. Install path is shown with ~/... notation.
qmd index prompt placeholder and default value display ~/... instead of the full home directory path.

Docs:

Added a Git backends section to the docs site covering GitHub and Cloudflare Artifacts setup.
Added llms.txt for LLM/AI crawler discoverability.
Docs site now generates .md files alongside HTML pages.

23.html:
Features:
  • Added the /brain-refresh and /brain-rebuild skills for re-embedding and redesigning the qmd index.
Refactors:
  • Unified every qmd path reference behind the __QMD_PATH__ placeholder and parallelized file patching during scaffold.
  • CLI version is now read from package.json instead of being hardcoded.
Fixes:
  • Resolved a batch of code smells and factual inconsistencies across the codebase.
  • patchVault now surfaces non-ENOENT errors instead of swallowing them.
  • Fixed a failing CI job.
Docs:
  • Added a unified mermaid architecture diagram to both READMEs.
  • Removed generic skills-marketplace boilerplate.

31.html: The wiki ships with a set of Claude Code slash commands that cover the full workflow. No manual prompting, no copy-pasting.
31.text: The wiki ships with a set of Claude Code slash commands that cover the full workflow. No manual prompting, no copy-pasting.
31.title: Skills

32.html:
• /brain-ingest — Drop a file into raw-sources/. Claude summarizes the source, creates a wiki/sources/ page, updates related topic pages, and flags contradictions.
  • /brain-search — Ask anything. Claude runs hybrid search, reads the most relevant pages, and writes an answer with inline [[wiki/page]] citations.
  • /lint — Health-check the wiki. Surfaces orphan pages, broken links, unresolved contradictions, and data gaps.
32.text:
/brain-ingest — Drop a file into raw-sources/. Claude summarizes the source, creates a wiki/sources/ page, updates related topic pages, and flags contradictions.
/brain-search — Ask anything. Claude runs hybrid search, reads the most relevant pages, and writes an answer with inline [[wiki/page]] citations.
/lint — Health-check the wiki. Surfaces orphan pages, broken links, unresolved contradictions, and data gaps.
32.title: Daily workflow

33.html:
• /brain-refresh — Re-scan the vault and regenerate vector embeddings for new or changed files.
  • /brain-rebuild — Destructive. Redesigns the qmd schema, drops the old index, and rebuilds from scratch. Use only when the current structure no longer fits how you search.
33.text:
/brain-refresh — Re-scan the vault and regenerate vector embeddings for new or changed files.
/brain-rebuild — Destructive. Redesigns the qmd schema, drops the old index, and rebuilds from scratch. Use only when the current structure no longer fits how you search.
33.title: Maintenance

34.html:
/brain-ingest, /brain-search, and /brain-refresh install globally to ~/.claude/skills/ during setup so they work in any Claude Code session. /brain-rebuild and /lint live inside the vault at .claude/skills/.

To pull in improvements from the latest template:

# Install or update all 5 wiki skills
npx skills add https://github.com/jessepinkman9900/claude-second-brain/tree/main/template/.claude/skills -a claude-code -y
 
# Or update a specific skill
npx skills add https://github.com/jessepinkman9900/claude-second-brain/tree/main/template/.claude/skills --skill brain-ingest -a claude-code -y

Once installed via npx skills, future updates are a single command:

npx skills update -a claude-code
34.text:
/brain-ingest, /brain-search, and /brain-refresh install globally to ~/.claude/skills/ during setup so they work in any Claude Code session. /brain-rebuild and /lint live inside the vault at .claude/skills/.
To pull in improvements from the latest template:
# Install or update all 5 wiki skills
npx skills add https://github.com/jessepinkman9900/claude-second-brain/tree/main/template/.claude/skills -a claude-code -y
 
# Or update a specific skill
npx skills add https://github.com/jessepinkman9900/claude-second-brain/tree/main/template/.claude/skills --skill brain-ingest -a claude-code -y
Once installed via npx skills, future updates are a single command:
npx skills update -a claude-code
34.title: Install or update skills

35.html:
Ingest a new source into the wiki.

Drop a file into raw-sources/articles/, raw-sources/pdfs/, or raw-sources/personal/, then run /brain-ingest. Claude will:

  1. Read the source in full.
  2. Summarize it in 3–5 bullets and ask you what matters most.
  3. Create a wiki/sources/[slug].md page with abstract, key claims, notable quotes, and your synthesis note.
  4. Update or create topic/entity pages that the source touches, integrating it as prose (not copy-paste) with source attribution.
  5. Flag contradictions with [!WARNING] callouts on any page where the new source disagrees with existing claims.
  6. Log the ingest in wiki/log.md.

Scope: global skill, installed at ~/.claude/skills/brain-ingest/SKILL.md. Paths resolve at call time via npx claude-second-brain path and npx claude-second-brain qmd — both read ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml to find the default brain. Source: template/.claude/skills/brain-ingest/SKILL.md.

35.title: /brain-ingest

36.html:
Destructive. Redesign the qmd schema from scratch.

Use only when the current collection/context structure no longer fits how you search — for example, when the wiki has grown into new domains that deserve their own collections.

What it does:

  1. Analyzes the current wiki.
  2. Proposes new collections and contexts.
  3. Waits for your approval before any destructive action.
  4. Patches scripts/qmd/setup.ts, drops the old index, and rebuilds embeddings from scratch.

Scope: vault-local, installed at .claude/skills/brain-rebuild/SKILL.md. Source: template/.claude/skills/brain-rebuild/SKILL.md.
36.text:

36.title: /brain-rebuild

37.html:
Refresh the qmd index.

Re-scans all collections for new or changed files and regenerates vector embeddings. Run after a bulk ingest session or manual edits to the wiki.

Pass force to re-embed every chunk — useful after changing the embedding model.

Scope: global skill, installed at ~/.claude/skills/brain-refresh/SKILL.md. Paths resolve at call time via npx claude-second-brain path and npx claude-second-brain qmd — both read ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml to find the default brain. Source: template/.claude/skills/brain-refresh/SKILL.md.

37.title: /brain-refresh

38.html:
Query the wiki to answer a question.

Runs hybrid search (vector + BM25) across the entire wiki, reads the 2–5 most relevant pages, and synthesizes an answer with inline [[wiki/page]] citations. Flags gaps where the wiki has no coverage.

If the answer pulls together multiple pages in a novel way, it offers to file it as a permanent wiki/qa/[slug].md entry — so the synthesis compounds next time.

Scope: global skill, installed at ~/.claude/skills/brain-search/SKILL.md. Paths resolve at call time via npx claude-second-brain path and npx claude-second-brain qmd — both read ~/.claude-second-brain/config.toml to find the default brain. Source: template/.claude/skills/brain-search/SKILL.md.

38.title: /brain-search

39.html:
Health-check the wiki.

Reports on:

  • Orphan pages — wiki pages with no inbound [[links]].
  • Unresolved contradictions — pages with [!WARNING] Contradiction callouts that haven't been resolved.
  • Stale claims — claims superseded by newer sources.
  • Missing pages — concepts/entities referenced by [[links]] that lack their own page.
  • Broken links — wikilinks pointing to files that don't exist.
  • Data gaps — important topics that lack source coverage; suggests specific sources to investigate.

Applies fixes where safe and flags everything else for your review. Appends a summary to wiki/log.md.

Scope: vault-local, installed at .claude/skills/lint/SKILL.md. Source: template/.claude/skills/lint/SKILL.md.

39.title: /lint

40.html: Cloudflare Artifacts is a git-compatible object storage remote. It's a good choice if you don't want to use GitHub — your vault data stays on Cloudflare's infrastructure and never touches GitHub.
40.text: Cloudflare Artifacts is a git-compatible object storage remote. It's a good choice if you don't want to use GitHub — your vault data stays on Cloudflare's infrastructure and never touches GitHub.
40.title: Cloudflare Artifacts remote

41.html:
• A Cloudflare account
  • wrangler CLI — if not installed, the setup wizard falls back to npx wrangler automatically
41.text:
A Cloudflare account
wrangler CLI — if not installed, the setup wizard falls back to npx wrangler automatically
41.title: Prerequisites

42.html:
When you run npx claude-second-brain and select Cloudflare Artifacts as your remote, the CLI handles everything:

  1. Checks wrangler auth (logs you in and requests the artifacts:write scope if needed)
  2. Creates a new Artifacts repo via the Cloudflare API
  3. Runs git remote add origin <remote-url>
  4. Pushes the initial commit
  5. Prints a short-lived repo token — save it
42.text:
When you run npx claude-second-brain and select Cloudflare Artifacts as your remote, the CLI handles everything:

Checks wrangler auth (logs you in and requests the artifacts:write scope if needed)
Creates a new Artifacts repo via the Cloudflare API
Runs git remote add origin <remote-url>
Pushes the initial commit
Prints a short-lived repo token — save it
42.title: Setup (automatic)

43.html:
After setup, the CLI prints a repo token:

Save your Artifacts repo token — it expires and you'll need to mint a new one:
  <token>

This token is used as the git credential for push and pull. It expires (default TTL: 24 hours). Save it somewhere — you'll need to mint a replacement when it expires.
43.text:
After setup, the CLI prints a repo token:
Save your Artifacts repo token — it expires and you'll need to mint a new one:
  <token>
This token is used as the git credential for push and pull. It expires (default TTL: 24 hours). Save it somewhere — you'll need to mint a replacement when it expires.
43.title: Saving your repo token

44.html:
Repo tokens expire, so mint a fresh one before each push/pull session:

# 1. Get your wrangler OAuth token
TOKEN=$(wrangler auth token)
 
# 2. Mint a new repo token (replace 'my-brain' and 'default' with your repo and namespace)
curl -X POST https://artifacts.cloudflare.net/v1/api/namespaces/default/tokens \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"repo":"my-brain","scope":"write","ttl":86400}'

The response contains a token field. Use it as a git credential:

git -c http.extraHeader="Authorization: Bearer <token>" push origin main
git -c http.extraHeader="Authorization: Bearer <token>" pull origin main
44.text:
Repo tokens expire, so mint a fresh one before each push/pull session:
# 1. Get your wrangler OAuth token
TOKEN=$(wrangler auth token)
 
# 2. Mint a new repo token (replace 'my-brain' and 'default' with your repo and namespace)
curl -X POST https://artifacts.cloudflare.net/v1/api/namespaces/default/tokens \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"repo":"my-brain","scope":"write","ttl":86400}'
The response contains a token field. Use it as a git credential:
git -c http.extraHeader="Authorization: Bearer <token>" push origin main
git -c http.extraHeader="Authorization: Bearer <token>" pull origin main
44.title: Pushing and pulling after setup

45.html: During setup, the CLI asks for an Artifacts namespace (default: default). Namespaces scope repos within your Cloudflare account — useful if you manage multiple projects. For personal use, the default is fine.
45.text: During setup, the CLI asks for an Artifacts namespace (default: default). Namespaces scope repos within your Cloudflare account — useful if you manage multiple projects. For personal use, the default is fine.
45.title: Namespace

46.html: GitHub is the default Git backend for claude-second-brain. Your vault is stored as a private GitHub repository — you get version history, access from any device, and Obsidian mobile sync via the bundled Git plugin.
46.text: GitHub is the default Git backend for claude-second-brain. Your vault is stored as a private GitHub repository — you get version history, access from any device, and Obsidian mobile sync via the bundled Git plugin.
46.title: GitHub remote

47.html:
• A GitHub account
  • gh CLI — used for authentication and repo creation during setup
47.text:
A GitHub account
gh CLI — used for authentication and repo creation during setup
47.title: Prerequisites

48.html:
When you run npx claude-second-brain and select GitHub as your remote, the CLI handles everything:

  1. Checks gh auth status — runs gh auth login if not authenticated
  2. Creates a private repo: gh repo create <name> --private --source=. --remote=origin --push
  3. Pushes the initial commit
48.text:
When you run npx claude-second-brain and select GitHub as your remote, the CLI handles everything:

Checks gh auth status — runs gh auth login if not authenticated
Creates a private repo: gh repo create <name> --private --source=. --remote=origin --push
Pushes the initial commit
48.title: Setup (automatic)

49.html:
If you skipped GitHub during setup, or gh wasn't installed, connect it manually:

# With gh CLI
gh repo create my-brain --private --source=. --remote=origin --push
 
# Without gh CLI
git remote add origin https://github.com/you/my-brain.git
git push -u origin main
49.text:
If you skipped GitHub during setup, or gh wasn't installed, connect it manually:
# With gh CLI
gh repo create my-brain --private --source=. --remote=origin --push
 
# Without gh CLI
git remote add origin https://github.com/you/my-brain.git
git push -u origin main
49.title: Manual setup

50.html:
The vault includes a pre-configured .obsidian/ folder with obsidian-git settings. Enable the plugin in Obsidian → Community plugins and it will auto-commit and push on a schedule.

For iOS: place the repo folder inside iCloud Drive — Obsidian Mobile picks it up natively and obsidian-git handles sync.
50.text:
The vault includes a pre-configured .obsidian/ folder with obsidian-git settings. Enable the plugin in Obsidian → Community plugins and it will auto-commit and push on a schedule.
For iOS: place the repo folder inside iCloud Drive — Obsidian Mobile picks it up natively and obsidian-git handles sync.
50.title: Syncing from Obsidian

51.html: Sources flow in on the left, Claude synthesizes them into the wiki, qmd indexes every page into a local hybrid search index, and GitHub makes the whole vault editable from Obsidian and Claude Code on any device.
51.text: Sources flow in on the left, Claude synthesizes them into the wiki, qmd indexes every page into a local hybrid search index, and GitHub makes the whole vault editable from Obsidian and Claude Code on any device.
51.title: How it works

52.html:
┌─────────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────────┐
│   Drop in a source  │        │ /brain-ingest       │        │     Wiki grows      │
│                     │        │                     │        │                     │
│  · article          │──────▶ │  reads + extracts   │──────▶ │  · cross-linked     │
│  · PDF              │        │  key knowledge      │        │  · contradictions   │
│  · personal note    │        │                     │        │    flagged          │
│                     │        │                     │        │  · syntheses        │
└─────────────────────┘        └─────────────────────┘        └─────────────────────┘

Query it anytime with /brain-search. Get answers with inline [[wiki/page]] citations, not a list of files.
52.text:
┌─────────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────────┐
│   Drop in a source  │        │ /brain-ingest       │        │     Wiki grows      │
│                     │        │                     │        │                     │
│  · article          │──────▶ │  reads + extracts   │──────▶ │  · cross-linked     │
│  · PDF              │        │  key knowledge      │        │  · contradictions   │
│  · personal note    │        │                     │        │    flagged          │
│                     │        │                     │        │  · syntheses        │
└─────────────────────┘        └─────────────────────┘        └─────────────────────┘
Query it anytime with /brain-search. Get answers with inline [[wiki/page]] citations, not a list of files.
52.title: The ingest loop, zoomed in

53.html:
my-brain/
├── CLAUDE.md              ← The schema. Claude reads this every session.
├── raw-sources/           ← Your raw inputs. Claude never modifies these.
│   ├── articles/
│   ├── pdfs/
│   └── personal/
├── wiki/                  ← Claude owns this entirely.
│   ├── index.md
│   ├── log.md
│   ├── overview.md
│   ├── sources/
│   └── qa/
└── scripts/qmd/           ← Semantic search setup and re-indexing
53.text:
my-brain/
├── CLAUDE.md              ← The schema. Claude reads this every session.
├── raw-sources/           ← Your raw inputs. Claude never modifies these.
│   ├── articles/
│   ├── pdfs/
│   └── personal/
├── wiki/                  ← Claude owns this entirely.
│   ├── index.md
│   ├── log.md
│   ├── overview.md
│   ├── sources/
│   └── qa/
└── scripts/qmd/           ← Semantic search setup and re-indexing
53.title: Directory layout

54.html: The authoritative schema lives in CLAUDE.md — Claude reads it at the start of every session. This page is a readable summary.
54.text: The authoritative schema lives in CLAUDE.md — Claude reads it at the start of every session. This page is a readable summary.
54.title: Wiki schema

55.html:
TypeFilePurpose
overviewwiki/overview.mdEvolving high-level synthesis
topicwiki/[concept].mdA concept, domain, or idea
entitywiki/[name].mdA person, tool, company, or project
source-summarywiki/sources/[slug].mdOne page per ingested source
qawiki/qa/[slug].mdFiled answers to notable queries

Plus two admin pages: wiki/index.md (master index) and wiki/log.md (running activity log).
55.text:
TypeFilePurposeoverviewwiki/overview.mdEvolving high-level synthesistopicwiki/[concept].mdA concept, domain, or ideaentitywiki/[name].mdA person, tool, company, or projectsource-summarywiki/sources/[slug].mdOne page per ingested sourceqawiki/qa/[slug].mdFiled answers to notable queries
Plus two admin pages: wiki/index.md (master index) and wiki/log.md (running activity log).
55.title: Page types

56.html:
Every wiki page has YAML frontmatter:

---
type: overview | topic | entity | source-summary | qa | admin
tags: [tag1, tag2]
sources: ["[[wiki/sources/source-slug]]"]
related: ["[[wiki/related-page]]"]
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
---
56.text:
Every wiki page has YAML frontmatter:
---
type: overview | topic | entity | source-summary | qa | admin
tags: [tag1, tag2]
sources: ["[[wiki/sources/source-slug]]"]
related: ["[[wiki/related-page]]"]
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
---
56.title: Frontmatter

57.html:
• File names — kebab-case, all lowercase. machine-learning-ops.md, not MachineLearningOps.md.
  • Links — always Obsidian [[wikilinks]] for internal cross-references. Full paths from vault root: [[wiki/distributed-systems]].
  • Sections — ## for top-level, ### for subsections.
  • Contradictions — flagged with a > [!WARNING] Contradiction callout. Both claims and their sources are stated.
  • Uncertainty — claims the model isn't sure about are marked with [?] inline.
57.text:
File names — kebab-case, all lowercase. machine-learning-ops.md, not MachineLearningOps.md.
Links — always Obsidian [[wikilinks]] for internal cross-references. Full paths from vault root: [[wiki/distributed-systems]].
Sections — ## for top-level, ### for subsections.
Contradictions — flagged with a > [!WARNING] Contradiction callout. Both claims and their sources are stated.
Uncertainty — claims the model isn't sure about are marked with [?] inline.
57.title: Naming conventions

58.html:
my-brain/
├── CLAUDE.md              ← The schema
├── raw-sources/           ← Immutable raw inputs
│   ├── articles/
│   ├── pdfs/
│   └── personal/
└── wiki/                  ← Claude-owned knowledge base
    ├── index.md
    ├── log.md
    ├── overview.md
    ├── sources/
    └── qa/
58.text:
my-brain/
├── CLAUDE.md              ← The schema
├── raw-sources/           ← Immutable raw inputs
│   ├── articles/
│   ├── pdfs/
│   └── personal/
└── wiki/                  ← Claude-owned knowledge base
    ├── index.md
    ├── log.md
    ├── overview.md
    ├── sources/
    └── qa/
58.title: Directory layout

59.html:
1. Nothing in raw-sources/ is ever modified — they are immutable raw inputs.
  2. Only wiki/ is owned by Claude; the rest of the vault is untouched.
  3. wiki/log.md is append-only, never overwritten.
  4. One source summary per ingested source in wiki/sources/ — never merged.
  5. Every wiki page has frontmatter with at least type and updated.
  6. Cross-link aggressively — if a topic has (or should have) a page, link it with [[wiki/page]].
  7. Pages are synthesis, not transcription.
59.text:
Nothing in raw-sources/ is ever modified — they are immutable raw inputs.
Only wiki/ is owned by Claude; the rest of the vault is untouched.
wiki/log.md is append-only, never overwritten.
One source summary per ingested source in wiki/sources/ — never merged.
Every wiki page has frontmatter with at least type and updated.
Cross-link aggressively — if a topic has (or should have) a page, link it with [[wiki/page]].
Pages are synthesis, not transcription.
59.title: Hard rules
